intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The execution tier of a two-agent "generator/executor" pattern — receives a code block from a paired LLM agent and runs it inside a configured environment (local host, Docker container, Jupyter kernel, container-hosted Jupyter, or Azure dynamic-sessions sandbox), returning stdout/stderr/exit status for the agent's next reasoning step. Every executor MUST confine file I/O to a configured work directory, enforce a wall-clock timeout, capture all output, isolate execution from the host caller's environment, and record every execution to an audit log keyed by timestamp / executor kind / language / source agent / work directory / timeout / exit status / code digest. The local executor in production, network egress beyond an allow-list, host-volume mounts outside the work directory, and any raising of CPU/memory/timeout ceilings all MUST require human approval.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Two Python packages — autogen-core (the abstract CodeExecutor base in code_executor/_base.py) and autogen-ext (five concrete executors under code_executors/: local, docker, docker_jupyter, jupyter, and azure). Each implements execute_code_blocks(), writing the code to a file in the work directory and invoking it via asyncio.create_subprocess_exec (local), container.exec_run with a timeout shell wrapper (docker), an in-process NotebookClient kernel (jupyter), a containerized Jupyter kernel gateway over WebSocket (docker_jupyter), or an HTTPS POST to an Azure Dynamic Sessions endpoint (azure). A top-level helper create_default_code_executor() in code_executors/__init__.py selects Docker if available and otherwise instantiates the local executor after emitting a UserWarning. There is no audit-log surface, no approval-gate surface, no work-directory enforcement on the Jupyter and Docker-Jupyter executors' code path, no container hardening on the Docker paths, and no environment scrubbing on the local path — every "MUST" in the remit's behavioral block is documentation, not enforced code.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is sandbox has the shape of isolation but not the substance. The remit declares six MUST clauses every executor must enforce — work-directory confinement, timeout, full output capture, host isolation, session-state non-leakage, and a structured audit log — and code in autogen-ext/code_executors implements only the second (timeout) consistently. LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies os.environ wholesale into the child process (local/__init__.py:397), gating safety on a one-shot warnings.warn with no approval path; DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates its container with no user=, no read_only=, no cap_drop=, no mem_limit=, no pids_limit=, and no network isolation (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550); create_default_code_executor() silently downgrades to the local path on any Docker failure (code_executors/__init__.py:58-80); DockerJupyterServer chmod 0o777s the bind mount (docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:328); and no executor anywhere in the tree writes an audit-log entry of any kind. Defaults the remit treats as forbidden are the defaults the code ships with.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Every code execution MUST be subject to a configured wall-clock timeout, and processes that exceed it MUST be terminated.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Stdout, stderr, and exit status MUST be captured completely and never silently discarded.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Persistence across executions MUST be limited to the configured state mechanism — preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors, work-directory files for command-line executors — and state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox, escalate privileges, or exfiltrate data MUST NOT be acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be loaded or executed from remote URLs or unverified sources on the LLM's behalf — the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any change to the executor's resource limits — CPU, memory, timeout — that raises the ceiling MUST require human approval.
No outbound network traffic from the executor itself (the executed code may make network calls if the sandbox permits, but the executor does not initiate its own)
The executor does not auto-upgrade, fetch dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration, or modify its own code
Code produced by the LLM agent(s) configured to pair with this executor in the AutoGen application

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the parent process's full os.environ into the executed subprocess, leaking credentials and configuration to LLM-generated code.
findings.description: At local/__init__.py:397 the executor calls env = os.environ.copy() and passes that directly as the subprocess environment, then optionally appends a virtual-env PATH prefix. There is no scrub, no allow-list, no warning that the executed code can read every environment variable in the host process — including OPENAI_API_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN, and anything else the parent loaded — directly violating the remit's "MUST NOT read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state" clause.
findings.policy_rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

evidence.snippet: `env = os.environ.copy()` — full parent environment copied into the subprocess with no filtering; the executed Python or shell script can `os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")` and exfiltrate it.
lines 399-400 — only modification is prepending the venv `bin_path` to `PATH`; nothing is removed.

recommended_actions: Replace the unconditional os.environ.copy() with an explicit allow-list of harmless variables (PATH, HOME, LANG, TERM, USER) and require operators to opt in additional variables via a constructor parameter.
Document the allow-list in the executor's docstring and emit a one-time warning when the operator passes additional variables.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with no user, capability, filesystem, memory, pid, or network hardening — the container runs as root with full default capabilities.
findings.description: At docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550 the executor calls client.containers.create with only image, name, entrypoint, command, tty, detach, auto_remove, volumes, working_dir, extra_hosts, and device_requests. There is no user= (so the default image user is used — root for python:3-slim), no read_only=True, no cap_drop=["ALL"], no mem_limit, no pids_limit, no cpu_quota, no network_disabled=True or network_mode, no security_opt=["no-new-privileges"]. The container is the operator's only line of defense against LLM-generated code, and it ships with every Docker default — including root, default network bridge, and unrestricted resource ceilings — directly violating "code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task."
findings.policy_rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

evidence.snippet: `self._container = await asyncio.to_thread(client.containers.create, ...)` — container creation block (lines 537-550) has no hardening keyword arguments.
default image is `python:3-slim`, whose `USER` is root.

recommended_actions: Pass safe defaults to containers.create: user="1000:1000", read_only=True, cap_drop=["ALL"], mem_limit="512m", pids_limit=256, network_disabled=True, security_opt=["no-new-privileges"]; make each one a constructor parameter so operators can relax individually, not all-or-nothing.
Add a constructor parameter network (default "none") that maps to network_mode, and require explicit opt-in for non-isolated networking.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor silently downgrades from Docker to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor when Docker is unavailable, emitting only a UserWarning with no approval gate.
findings.description: At code_executors/__init__.py:58-80 the helper checks _is_docker_available(); if true it returns the Docker executor, and if false (or if Docker construction raises any Exception — the except Exception: pass at line 64-66 silently swallows it) it falls through to emit a single warnings.warn(..., UserWarning) and instantiate LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor. The remit names "use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval" as a hard gate — a UserWarning is not an approval gate, and operators have no way to require the helper to fail instead of degrade.
findings.policy_rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

evidence.snippet: lines 64-66 — `except Exception: pass` — any Docker initialization failure (image not pullable, permissions, network) is silently swallowed and the helper continues to the local fallback.
lines 69-75 — fallback emits `warnings.warn(..., UserWarning, stacklevel=2)` and returns LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor; no operator confirmation is required.

recommended_actions: Add a constructor parameter allow_local_fallback: bool = False; when False (the default), raise RuntimeError("Docker unavailable; refusing to downgrade to local executor") instead of falling back.
When the operator explicitly opts in to local fallback, require them to also pass i_understand_local_execution=True so the decision is auditable in their own code.

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor and DockerJupyterCodeExecutor execute arbitrary LLM-supplied code in a kernel with no work-directory confinement check — get_file_name_from_content is never called on these paths.
findings.description: JupyterCodeExecutor._execute_code_block at jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:189-241 and DockerJupyterCodeExecutor.execute_code_blocks at docker_jupyter/_docker_jupyter.py:197-246 both pass the raw code string straight to nbformat.new_code_cell / kernel_client.execute without invoking get_file_name_from_content or any work-directory enforcement. The kernel can then call open("/etc/passwd"), write to anywhere the kernel user can reach, and (for the plain Jupyter executor) read/write the host filesystem unrestricted. The remit's "MUST be confined to a configured work directory" clause is enforced on the command-line executors but not on the Jupyter executors.
findings.policy_rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected.

evidence.snippet: lines 200-207 — code is wrapped only via `silence_pip` and passed to `nbformat.new_code_cell`; no path check, no chroot, no chdir to a confined directory before execution.
lines 221-225 — `code = silence_pip(code_block.code, code_block.language)` then immediately `kernel_client.execute(code, ...)`; the kernel inherits the container's full filesystem view.

recommended_actions: For JupyterCodeExecutor, configure the NotebookClient with a resources={'metadata': {'path': str(self.work_dir)}} argument and additionally chdir via a pre-cell so the kernel cannot trivially escape; document that JupyterCodeExecutor is non-isolation and direct callers to the Docker variant for untrusted code.

For DockerJupyterCodeExecutor, ensure the underlying DockerJupyterServer mounts only the configured work_dir into the container and rejects any extra bind via the same approval-gated parameter as DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer chmods the operator-supplied bind directory to 0o777 — world-writable — on every server start.
findings.description: At docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:325-328, when the operator passes a bind_dir, the server calls os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) unconditionally. This makes the host directory world-writable to any user on the host system, opening the work directory to local privilege escalation by any non-root user (including the agent process itself if it runs unprivileged) and contradicting the remit's host-isolation clause. The kernel running in the container also has full read/write to the same host path through the bind mount, so anything the kernel writes is then writable by any host user.
findings.policy_rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

evidence.snippet: lines 325-328 — `if bind_dir: self._bind_dir = Path(bind_dir) ...; self._bind_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True); os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777)` — unconditional world-writable chmod on the host bind directory.

recommended_actions: Remove the os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) entirely; let the operator manage permissions on a directory they own.
If the kernel user inside the container needs to write the directory, run the container with user="$(id -u):$(id -g)" matching the host user instead of widening host permissions.

findings.summary: No executor writes an audit log — the remit's required per-execution record (timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, code digest) is entirely unimplemented.
findings.description: A repository-wide search across autogen-ext/code_executors/ and autogen-core/code_executor/ returns zero matches for audit, and the only logging calls are logging.info on pip-install setup and logging.error on Docker stop/cleanup. sha256 is computed on the code (local __init__.py:381, docker _docker_code_executor.py:353) but only as a temp-filename component — it is never written to any record. The remit names a structured audit log as one of the six MUST clauses; the field that would carry the digest already exists in working code but is discarded.
findings.policy_rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: `grep -rn audit` returns no matches across the entire executors tree.
`sha256(code.encode()).hexdigest()` is computed and used only for a filename, never persisted to an audit record.
same pattern — sha256 used for filename, no audit-log write.

recommended_actions: Add a structured audit hook to the CodeExecutor base class — a method like _record_execution(self, kind, language, source, work_dir, timeout, exit_code, code_digest) — and invoke it from each concrete executor at the start and end of execute_code_blocks.
Default the hook to write a single JSON line to a configurable path (or to a structured Python logger with a named "audit" logger), and document the field schema.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor's docstring claims command-line code is "sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands" but no such regex or dangerous-command list exists in the file.
findings.description: The class docstring at local/__init__.py:57 reads: "Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands in order to prevent self-destructive commands from being executed which may potentially affect the users environment." There is no such code in the module — `grep -n "dangerous\|sanitize"` returns nothing. The only regex-based processing is silence_pip (adds -qqq to pip install lines). The documented control is fictional, giving operators and the remit's "instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox MUST NOT be acted on" clause a false signal that a guardrail exists.
findings.policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox, escalate privileges, or exfiltrate data MUST NOT be acted on.

evidence.snippet: lines 57-59 — docstring claims regex-based sanitization against a dangerous-command list.
no regex matching against a deny-list of commands is present anywhere in the file; only `silence_pip` from `_common.py` is invoked, which strictly adds `-qqq` to pip-install lines.

recommended_actions: Remove the false claim from the docstring — say plainly that LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor performs no command-content sanitization and direct the operator to the Docker variant for untrusted code.

Optionally, implement an opt-in deny-list (constructor parameter denied_command_patterns) so operators who want a soft guardrail can enable one; do not make it default-on, as a deny-list is incomplete by construction.

findings.summary: ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor.download_files writes a remote-API-supplied filename directly to local disk under work_dir with no path-traversal check.
findings.description: At azure/_azure_container_code_executor.py:354-391, download_files iterates the operator-requested file list, verifies each exists in get_file_list (which returns whatever the Azure API responds with at line 307), and then writes self.work_dir / file with no resolve()/relative_to(work_dir) check. A compromised or misconfigured Azure session returning a filename like ../../etc/cron.daily/x in get_file_list would be written outside the work directory. This violates the remit's "work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including ... absolute paths" clause.
findings.policy_rule_text: Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

evidence.snippet: `local_path = self.work_dir / file` — direct join with no path validation; `file` is whatever appeared in `get_file_list`'s response.
lines 381-382 — write proceeds unconditionally with the joined path.

recommended_actions: After computing local_path, call local_path.resolve().relative_to(self.work_dir.resolve()) and raise ValueError if the file path escapes work_dir.
Apply the same check to _common.get_file_name_from_content's sibling logic — the helper already does this for inbound code; mirror it for inbound download filenames.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor leaves the container on Docker's default bridge network — no network isolation, no egress allow-list, no approval surface for network access.
findings.description: At docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550 the container is created without network_disabled=True, without network_mode="none", and without a constructor parameter to configure either. The default Docker bridge network has full outbound internet access. The remit explicitly states "no outbound network traffic from the executor itself" and "enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval" — both are violated by the shipped defaults. LLM-generated code can call requests.get, curl, or open a reverse shell with no impediment.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration. / Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval. / No outbound network traffic from the executor itself (the executed code may make network calls if the sandbox permits, but the executor does not initiate its own)

evidence.snippet: `containers.create` call (lines 537-550) has no `network_disabled` or `network_mode` argument; the container inherits the default bridge network.
`DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig` (lines 68-82) has no network field.

recommended_actions: Add a constructor parameter network: str = "none"; pass it as network_mode to containers.create; require operators to explicitly set it to a non-none value, log a warning when they do, and document the network-policy expectation.
Add a sibling parameter egress_allow_list: Optional[List[str]] that (in a future version) configures an iptables/nftables sidecar or relies on a user-defined Docker network with restricted egress.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor accepts arbitrary host volumes via extra_volumes with no validation that they live under the configured work directory.
findings.description: The extra_volumes constructor parameter at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:177 is documented as "extra volumes (beyond the work_dir) to mount to the container" with an example mounting /home/user1/ and /var/www. At line 546 the dictionary is spread directly into the volumes argument of containers.create with no allow-list, no path normalization, and no check that the operator-supplied source paths are inside the work directory. The remit explicitly names this case: "Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval" — no approval surface exists, the operator just passes a dict.
findings.policy_rule_text: Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: `extra_volumes` accepted as constructor parameter (also line 232) with no validation.
`volumes={str(self.bind_dir.resolve()): {"bind": "/workspace", "mode": "rw"}, **self._extra_volumes}` — extra volumes spread directly into the container spec.

recommended_actions: Rename extra_volumes to _extra_volumes_unsafe and require an additional i_understand_extra_volumes=True parameter at construction time when any extra volumes are supplied.
Log an audit-level entry naming each extra volume's source/destination at start time so post-hoc review can detect the case.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor concatenates stdout and stderr into a single output string and drops the distinction; on container-side timeout it appends "\n Timeout" with no exit-code differentiation.
findings.description: At docker/_docker_code_executor.py:303-306, _execute_command returns a single combined output string from container.exec_run (which folds stdout and stderr together by default) — the caller cannot tell which bytes came from which stream. On container-side timeout (exit 124, line 304-305) the executor appends "\n Timeout" to the same blob. The remit's "stdout, stderr, and exit status MUST be captured completely and never silently discarded" plus "error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller" are both partially violated — bytes survive but the stderr/stdout boundary does not.
findings.policy_rule_text: Stdout, stderr, and exit status MUST be captured completely and never silently discarded. / Error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller.

evidence.snippet: lines 296-306 — `exec_task = asyncio.create_task(asyncio.to_thread(self._container.exec_run, command))` then `output = result.output.decode("utf-8")` — `exec_run` defaults to combining streams; no `demux=True` is passed.
lines 237-238 — `outputs.append(json.dumps(data.data))` for non-text/non-image data types — silently transforms structured output into JSON without flagging.

recommended_actions: Pass demux=True to exec_run and propagate stdout / stderr as separate fields on CommandLineCodeResult; extend the dataclass with stderr: str.
Add an explicit timed_out: bool field on the result dataclass instead of relying on exit code 124 appearing alongside "\n Timeout" prose.

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor and DockerJupyterCodeExecutor share kernel state across all execute_code_blocks calls with no operator-visible session-isolation guarantee beyond an explicit restart() call.
findings.description: Both Jupyter-style executors hold a single kernel client (jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:159, docker_jupyter/_docker_jupyter.py:185) and only reset state when restart() is invoked. The remit accepts persistence across executions for Jupyter executors but says "state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions" — there is no surfaced concept of a "session" boundary at the executor API, so the caller must remember to call restart() between unrelated agent runs. If two distinct AutoGen application sessions reuse the same executor instance, the second sees variables, imported modules, and any side-effects from the first.
findings.policy_rule_text: Persistence across executions MUST be limited to the configured state mechanism — preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors, work-directory files for command-line executors — and state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions.

evidence.snippet: lines 268-271 — `restart()` does `stop()` then `start()`; nothing else marks session boundaries.
lines 248-256 — restart is operator-driven; nothing in `execute_code_blocks` checks for a new session id.

recommended_actions: Add a session_id constructor parameter and an internal check in execute_code_blocks that calls restart when the supplied session_id changes between calls.
Document explicitly that re-using a Jupyter executor across unrelated sessions without restart() shares state.

findings.summary: silence_pip and the executors accept and execute pip-install lines in agent-supplied code, fetching arbitrary packages from PyPI at runtime — the remit's "MUST NOT be loaded from remote URLs" intent is bypassed.
findings.description: _common.silence_pip at lines 114-131 detects ! pip install / pip install lines in agent-supplied code and only adds -qqq; the line still executes and pip will fetch any operator-or-LLM-named package from any configured index. The remit says "code MUST NOT be loaded or executed from remote URLs or unverified sources on the LLM's behalf — the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input" — pip install lines load and execute setup.py / wheel postinstall code from PyPI, which is exactly remote-source execution.
findings.policy_rule_text: Code MUST NOT be loaded or executed from remote URLs or unverified sources on the LLM's behalf — the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input.

evidence.snippet: lines 114-131 — `silence_pip` detects pip install lines and adds `-qqq`; lines still execute.
`code = silence_pip(code, lang)` — no executor blocks or audits pip install lines (same pattern in docker, jupyter, docker_jupyter).

recommended_actions: Add a constructor parameter allow_pip_install: bool = False; when False, the executors reject any code block containing a pip-install line with a structured error.
Where pip install is permitted, require the operator to also pass an allowed_packages allow-list and refuse package names not on the list.

findings.summary: Executor timeouts and resource ceilings are constructor parameters with no minimum-ceiling enforcement — any caller can raise timeouts to arbitrary values with no audit trail.
findings.description: Each executor accepts a timeout constructor parameter (local __init__.py:171, docker _docker_code_executor.py:182, jupyter _jupyter_code_executor.py:145, docker_jupyter _docker_jupyter.py:165, azure _azure_container_code_executor.py:108) with only a timeout >= 1 floor; there is no upper bound, no central policy, no audit-log entry naming the chosen value, and no approval gate. Memory and CPU ceilings on Docker are absent entirely (see PRAX-2026-05-25-002). The remit's "any change to the executor's resource limits — CPU, memory, timeout — that raises the ceiling MUST require human approval" is unenforceable because the executor has no notion of a baseline ceiling against which a raise could be detected.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any change to the executor's resource limits — CPU, memory, timeout — that raises the ceiling MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: lines 171-172 — `if timeout < 1: raise ValueError("Timeout must be greater than or equal to 1.")` — only floor enforcement (same pattern in docker line 182-183).
lines 537-550 — no `mem_limit` / `cpu_quota` / `pids_limit` arguments anywhere.

recommended_actions: Introduce a class-level MAX_TIMEOUT constant and require explicit override via a separate parameter to exceed it; log every override at audit level (see PRAX-2026-05-25-006).
Add Docker mem_limit, cpu_quota, pids_limit constructor parameters with safe defaults (paired with PRAX-2026-05-25-002).

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor pulls images by floating tag and bundled DockerJupyterServer image pulls upstream base without digest pinning — supply-chain integrity is not enforced.
findings.description: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig.image defaults to "python:3-slim" (a floating tag; docker/_docker_code_executor.py:71) and DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.start calls client.images.pull(self._image) at line 524 with no digest check; DockerJupyterServer.DEFAULT_DOCKERFILE begins FROM quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation with no digest (docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:267). The remit's out-of-scope clause "the executor does not auto-upgrade, fetch dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration" is violated — every pull at runtime is dependency-fetch from a registry without explicit configuration.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor does not auto-upgrade, fetch dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration, or modify its own code

evidence.snippet: `image: str = "python:3-slim"` — floating tag default.
lines 520-524 — `await asyncio.to_thread(client.images.pull, self._image)` — pull on first use, no digest verification.
`FROM quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation` — base image not digest-pinned.

recommended_actions: Change the default to a digest-pinned image (e.g., python:3.12-slim@sha256:...) and document the upgrade procedure.
Validate that any operator-supplied image string contains @sha256: by default; allow an opt-out flag for development.

findings.summary: The DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor init_command parameter is shell-concatenated into the container entrypoint with no validation — operator-supplied, but a shell-injection shape that will fire if reached from configuration.
findings.description: At docker/_docker_code_executor.py:528, command = ["-c", f"{(self._init_command)};exec {shell_command}"] if self._init_command else None — the operator-supplied init_command is concatenated into a /bin/sh -c string with a literal ; separator. The parameter is operator-controlled, not LLM-controlled, so the practical risk is bounded — but the same parameter is exposed in DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig, and if the config is hydrated from JSON / YAML / a UI form that an LLM influences, this becomes a shell-injection path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Code produced by the LLM agent(s) configured to pair with this executor in the AutoGen application

evidence.snippet: `command = ["-c", f"{(self._init_command)};exec {shell_command}"] if self._init_command else None` — string concatenation with literal `;`.
`init_command` is a config field (also line 233); downstream operator code may sometimes hydrate it from untrusted sources.

recommended_actions: Replace the string-concatenated init_command with a list-of-arguments form; validate the operator-supplied value at construction time and reject embedded shell metacharacters (;, &, |, backticks, $().
Document that init_command is operator-trusted input and must never be hydrated from LLM output.

findings.summary: JupyterClient.get_kernel_client always opens the WebSocket as ws:// even when JupyterConnectionInfo.use_https=True, transmitting kernel traffic and auth token in cleartext when paired with a remote gateway.
findings.description: JupyterClient._get_ws_base_url at docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:78-80 hard-codes protocol = "ws"; _get_api_base_url at lines 73-76 correctly honours use_https with "https". So a connection to a remote Jupyter gateway authenticates the HTTP API call over TLS but then opens the persistent kernel WebSocket — carrying the code, stdin/stdout, and the bearer token in the headers — over cleartext ws://. The current first-party server runs on 127.0.0.1 so the practical risk is low, but the shape is wrong and will leak if the operator points the client at a remote gateway.

evidence.snippet: lines 78-80 — `def _get_ws_base_url(self) -> str: port = ...; return f"ws://{self._connection_info.host}{port}"` — protocol is hard-coded `ws`.
lines 122-126 — `ws = await websockets.connect(ws_url, additional_headers=self._get_headers())` — bearer token in `Authorization` header sent over cleartext.

recommended_actions: Mirror _get_api_base_url's protocol selection: protocol = "wss" if self._connection_info.use_https else "ws".
Add a unit test for the URL builder that asserts both protocols are honoured.

positives.title: Wall-clock timeout consistently enforced
positives.description: Every executor enforces a configurable timeout with a floor of 1 second, and the command-line variants terminate the subprocess (or rely on the Docker timeout wrapper) on expiry. The remit's "every code execution MUST be subject to a configured wall-clock timeout" clause is the one MUST that is honoured everywhere.

positives.title: Workspace-relative filename check is correctly implemented in _common.get_file_name_from_content
positives.description: The helper at _common.py:96-111 resolves the supplied filename against the workspace and calls Path.relative_to(workspace_path.resolve()), which raises ValueError on parent-directory or absolute-path escape. The command-line executors call it and return a structured error on escape attempts — this is the right shape, and the Jupyter / Azure paths should reuse it.

positives.title: Cancellation tokens are wired through every executor
positives.description: Every execute_code_blocks implementation accepts a CancellationToken and uses cancellation_token.link_future(task) so operator-initiated cancel propagates to the running subprocess / container exec / kernel call. This gives the operator application a clean halt path.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files are produced by the executor subsystem itself; the audit-log absence is itself a finding (PRAX-2026-05-25-006).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The subsystem has a coherent abstraction (work directory, timeout, structured CodeResult) and the Docker / Azure / Docker-Jupyter paths do place execution in a separate process / container / kernel — so isolation is not nil — but every dimension the remit names as a MUST (audit logging, host-environment isolation on the local path, container hardening, approval for local-in-production, work-directory enforcement on the Jupyter paths, network egress controls) is absent or defaulted off. Supply Chain is weak (Docker image pinned only by floating tag python:3-slim, the bundled Jupyter Dockerfile pulls quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation unpinned) and adversarial-testing evidence in scope is zero. Monitoring is zero — there is no audit-log surface at all.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The subsystem is scoped to a single capability — execute a CodeBlock and return a CodeResult — and the abstract base in autogen-core/_base.py exposes only execute_code_blocks, start, stop, restart; that bounding is genuine. But within that scope individual executors expose broad escape hatches (operator-supplied extra_volumes in Docker, init_command shell-concatenated into the container entrypoint, no language allow-list enforcement on the Azure path beyond "python"), so the domain bound is shape-only.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The executor does not itself ingest external content into an LLM context — code arrives from the calling agent and only stdout/stderr/exit-status flow back — but it also does no content-origin labeling on the returned output and no filtering before that output is handed back to the LLM, so an attacker who controls the executed code controls the next-turn context unchecked. The remit's "no executor-initiated network egress" clause is structurally true for the Python code but operationally void in Docker (no network_disabled=True, default bridge network).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The remit's six MUST clauses (work-dir confinement, timeout, output capture, host isolation, state non-leakage, audit log) are implemented as documentation rather than code on most paths — LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor warns and proceeds with full os.environ.copy() at line 397 with no approval gate; DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor ships zero container-hardening flags (no user, no read_only, no cap_drop, no mem_limit, no network isolation) at lines 537-550; JupyterCodeExecutor runs code in an in-process kernel on the local host with no warning and no work-dir confinement; and create_default_code_executor silently falls back to local on any Docker error. Timeouts are the one consistently enforced control.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The default Docker image in DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig is pinned to the floating tag python:3-slim and the bundled Jupyter image pulls quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation without a digest (docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:267); images are pulled on first use via client.images.pull with no integrity verification; there is no ML-BOM or pinned-digest manifest for the runtime container surface — re-pull at any time can swap the execution environment under the operator with no signal.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Nothing in scope — no tests/ directory under the two packages we read, no fixtures exercising sandbox-escape, no documented adversarial review of the executor surface, no fuzz harness against get_file_name_from_content or infer_lang. Score is Absent for the in-scope tree; raising it would need evidence of an adversarial exercise that produced architectural change in this surface specifically.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No executor writes an audit-log entry — grep -rn audit returns nothing across both trees, and the only logging calls are logging.info on pip-install setup and logging.error on Docker stop/cleanup. The remit's audit-log requirement (timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, code digest) is unimplemented; sha256 is computed but only as a temp-filename component, never written to a record.
